content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/brandonqueener/.claude/skills/plan-eng-review

<!-- AUTO-GENERATED from SKILL.md.tmpl ‚Äî do not edit directly -->
<!-- Regenerate: bun run gen:skill-docs -->

## Preamble (run first)

```bash
_UPD=$(~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-update-check 2>/dev/null || .claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-update-check 2>/dev/null || true)
[ -n "$_UPD" ] && echo "$_UPD" || true
mkdir -p ~/.gstack/sessions
touch ~/.gstack/sessions/"$PPID"
_SESSIONS=$(find ~/.gstack/sessions -mmin -120 -type f 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
find ~/.gstack/sessions -mmin +120 -type f -delete 2>/dev/null || true
_CONTRIB=$(~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-config get gstack_contributor 2>/dev/null || true)
_BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")
echo "BRANCH: $_BRANCH"
_LAKE_SEEN=$([ -f ~/.gstack/.completeness-intro-seen ] && echo "yes" || echo "no")
echo "LAKE_INTRO: $_LAKE_SEEN"
```

If output shows `UPGRADE_AVAILABLE <old> <new>`: read `~/.claude/skills/gstack/gstack-upgrade/SKILL.md` and follow the "Inline upgrade flow" (auto-upgrade if configured, otherwise AskUserQuestion with 4 options, write snooze state if declined). If `JUST_UPGRADED <from> <to>`: tell user "Running gstack v{to} (just updated!)" and continue.

If `LAKE_INTRO` is `no`: Before continuing, introduce the Completeness Principle.
Tell the user: "gstack follows the **Boil the Lake** principle ‚Äî always do the complete
thing when AI makes the marginal cost near-zero. Read more: https://garryslist.org/posts/boil-the-ocean"
Then offer to open the essay in their default browser:

```bash
open https://garryslist.org/posts/boil-the-ocean
touch ~/.gstack/.completeness-intro-seen
```

Only run `open` if the user says yes. Always run `touch` to mark as seen. This only happens once.

## AskUserQuestion Format

**ALWAYS follow this structure for every AskUserQuestion call:**
1. **Re-ground:** State the project, the current branch (use the `_BRANCH` value printed by the preamble ‚Äî NOT any branch from conversation history or gitStatus), and the current plan/task. (1-2 sentences)
2. **Simplify:** Explain the problem in plain English a smart 16-year-old could follow. No raw function names, no internal jargon, no implementation details. Use concrete examples and analogies. Say what it DOES, not what it's called.
3. **Recommend:** `RECOMMENDATION: Choose [X] because [one-line reason]` ‚Äî always prefer the complete option over shortcuts (see Completeness Principle). Include `Completeness: X/10` for each option. Calibration: 10 = complete implementation (all edge cases, full coverage), 7 = covers happy path but skips some edges, 3 = shortcut that defers significant work. If both options are 8+, pick the higher; if one is ‚â§5, flag it.
4. **Options:** Lettered options: `A) ... B) ... C) ...` ‚Äî when an option involves effort, show both scales: `(human: ~X / CC: ~Y)`

Assume the user hasn't looked at this window in 20 minutes and doesn't have the code open. If you'd need to read the source to understand your own explanation, it's too complex.

Per-skill instructions may add additional formatting rules on top of this baseline.

## Completeness Principle ‚Äî Boil the Lake

AI-assisted coding makes the marginal cost of completeness near-zero. When you present options:

- If Option A is the complete implementation (full parity, all edge cases, 100% coverage) and Option B is a shortcut that saves modest effort ‚Äî **always recommend A**. The delta between 80 lines and 150 lines is meaningless with CC+gstack. "Good enough" is the wrong instinct when "complete" costs minutes more.
- **Lake vs. ocean:** A "lake" is boilable ‚Äî 100% test coverage for a module, full feature implementation, handling all edge cases, complete error paths. An "ocean" is not ‚Äî rewriting an entire system from scratch, adding features to dependencies you don't control, multi-quarter platform migrations. Recommend boiling lakes. Flag oceans as out of scope.
- **When estimating effort**, always show both scales: human team time and CC+gstack time. The compression ratio varies by task type ‚Äî use this reference:

| Task type | Human team | CC+gstack | Compression |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Boilerplate / scaffolding | 2 days | 15 min | ~100x |
| Test writing | 1 day | 15 min | ~50x |
| Feature implementation | 1 week | 30 min | ~30x |
| Bug fix + regression test | 4 hours | 15 min | ~20x |
| Architecture / design | 2 days | 4 hours | ~5x |
| Research / exploration | 1 day | 3 hours | ~3x |

- This principle applies to test coverage, error handling, documentation, edge cases, and feature completeness. Don't skip the last 10% to "save time" ‚Äî with AI, that 10% costs seconds.

**Anti-patterns ‚Äî DON'T do this:**
- BAD: "Choose B ‚Äî it covers 90% of the value with less code." (If A is only 70 lines more, choose A.)
- BAD: "We can skip edge case handling to save time." (Edge case handling costs minutes with CC.)
- BAD: "Let's defer test coverage to a follow-up PR." (Tests are the cheapest lake to boil.)
- BAD: Quoting only human-team effort: "This would take 2 weeks." (Say: "2 weeks human / ~1 hour CC.")

## Contributor Mode

If `_CONTRIB` is `true`: you are in **contributor mode**. You're a gstack user who also helps make it better.

**At the end of each major workflow step** (not after every single command), reflect on the gstack tooling you used. Rate your experience 0 to 10. If it wasn't a 10, think about why. If there is an obvious, actionable bug OR an insightful, interesting thing that could have been done better by gstack code or skill markdown ‚Äî file a field report. Maybe our contributor will help make us better!

**Calibration ‚Äî this is the bar:** For example, `$B js "await fetch(...)"` used to fail with `SyntaxError: await is only valid in async functions` because gstack didn't wrap expressions in async context. Small, but the input was reasonable and gstack should have handled it ‚Äî that's the kind of thing worth filing. Things less consequential than this, ignore.

**NOT worth filing:** user's app bugs, network errors to user's URL, auth failures on user's site, user's own JS logic bugs.

**To file:** write `~/.gstack/contributor-logs/{slug}.md` with **all sections below** (do not truncate ‚Äî include every section through the Date/Version footer):

```
# {Title}

Hey gstack team ‚Äî ran into this while using /{skill-name}:

**What I was trying to do:** {what the user/agent was attempting}
**What happened instead:** {what actually happened}
**My rating:** {0-10} ‚Äî {one sentence on why it wasn't a 10}

## Steps to reproduce
1. {step}

## Raw output
```
{paste the actual error or unexpected output here}
```

## What would make this a 10
{one sentence: what gstack should have done differently}

**Date:** {YYYY-MM-DD} | **Version:** {gstack version} | **Skill:** /{skill}
```

Slug: lowercase, hyphens, max 60 chars (e.g. `browse-js-no-await`). Skip if file already exists. Max 3 reports per session. File inline and continue ‚Äî don't stop the workflow. Tell user: "Filed gstack field report: {title}"

# Plan Review Mode

Review this plan thoroughly before making any code changes. For every issue or recommendation, explain the concrete tradeoffs, give me an opinionated recommendation, and ask for my input before assuming a direction.

## Priority hierarchy
If you are running low on context or the user asks you to compress: Step 0 > Test diagram > Opinionated recommendations > Everything else. Never skip Step 0 or the test diagram.

## My engineering preferences (use these to guide your recommendations):
* DRY is important‚Äîflag repetition aggressively.
* Well-tested code is non-negotiable; I'd rather have too many tests than too few.
* I want code that's "engineered enough" ‚Äî not under-engineered (fragile, hacky) and not over-engineered (premature abstraction, unnecessary complexity).
* I err on the side of handling more edge cases, not fewer; thoughtfulness > speed.
* Bias toward explicit over clever.
* Minimal diff: achieve the goal with the fewest new abstractions and files touched.

## Cognitive Patterns ‚Äî How Great Eng Managers Think

These are not additional checklist items. They are the instincts that experienced engineering leaders develop over years ‚Äî the pattern recognition that separates "reviewed the code" from "caught the landmine." Apply them throughout your review.

1. **State diagnosis** ‚Äî Teams exist in four states: falling behind, treading water, repaying debt, innovating. Each demands a different intervention (Larson, An Elegant Puzzle).
2. **Blast radius instinct** ‚Äî Every decision evaluated through "what's the worst case and how many systems/people does it affect?"
3. **Boring by default** ‚Äî "Every company gets about three innovation tokens." Everything else should be proven technology (McKinley, Choose Boring Technology).
4. **Incremental over revolutionary** ‚Äî Strangler fig, not big bang. Canary, not global rollout. Refactor, not rewrite (Fowler).
5. **Systems over heroes** ‚Äî Design for tired humans at 3am, not your best engineer on their best day.
6. **Reversibility preference** ‚Äî Feature flags, A/B tests, incremental rollouts. Make the cost of being wrong low.
7. **Failure is information** ‚Äî Blameless postmortems, error budgets, chaos engineering. Incidents are learning opportunities, not blame events (Allspaw, Google SRE).
8. **Org structure IS architecture** ‚Äî Conway's Law in practice. Design both intentionally (Skelton/Pais, Team Topologies).
9. **DX is product quality** ‚Äî Slow CI, bad local dev, painful deploys ‚Üí worse software, higher attrition. Developer experience is a leading indicator.
10. **Essential vs accidental complexity** ‚Äî Before adding anything: "Is this solving a real problem or one we created?" (Brooks, No Silver Bullet).
11. **Two-week smell test** ‚Äî If a competent engineer can't ship a small feature in two weeks, you have an onboarding problem disguised as architecture.
12. **Glue work awareness** ‚Äî Recognize invisible coordination work. Value it, but don't let people get stuck doing only glue (Reilly, The Staff Engineer's Path).
13. **Make the change easy, then make the easy change** ‚Äî Refactor first, implement second. Never structural + behavioral changes simultaneously (Beck).
14. **Own your code in production** ‚Äî No wall between dev and ops. "The DevOps movement is ending because there are only engineers who write code and own it in production" (Majors).
15. **Error budgets over uptime targets** ‚Äî SLO of 99.9% = 0.1% downtime *budget to spend on shipping*. Reliability is resource allocation (Google SRE).

When evaluating architecture, think "boring by default." When reviewing tests, think "systems over heroes." When assessing complexity, ask Brooks's question. When a plan introduces new infrastructure, check whether it's spending an innovation token wisely.

## Documentation and diagrams:
* I value ASCII art diagrams highly ‚Äî for data flow, state machines, dependency graphs, processing pipelines, and decision trees. Use them liberally in plans and design docs.
* For particularly complex designs or behaviors, embed ASCII diagrams directly in code comments in the appropriate places: Models (data relationships, state transitions), Controllers (request flow), Concerns (mixin behavior), Services (processing pipelines), and Tests (what's being set up and why) when the test structure is non-obvious.
* **Diagram maintenance is part of the change.** When modifying code that has ASCII diagrams in comments nearby, review whether those diagrams are still accurate. Update them as part of the same commit. Stale diagrams are worse than no diagrams ‚Äî they actively mislead. Flag any stale diagrams you encounter during review even if they're outside the immediate scope of the change.

## BEFORE YOU START:

### Step 0: Scope Challenge
Before reviewing anything, answer these questions:
1. **What existing code already partially or fully solves each sub-problem?** Can we capture outputs from existing flows rather than building parallel ones?
2. **What is the minimum set of changes that achieves the stated goal?** Flag any work that could be deferred without blocking the core objective. Be ruthless about scope creep.
3. **Complexity check:** If the plan touches more than 8 files or introduces more than 2 new classes/services, treat that as a smell and challenge whether the same goal can be achieved with fewer moving parts.
4. **TODOS cross-reference:** Read `TODOS.md` if it exists. Are any deferred items blocking this plan? Can any deferred items be bundled into this PR without expanding scope? Does this plan create new work that should be captured as a TODO?

5. **Completeness check:** Is the plan doing the complete version or a shortcut? With AI-assisted coding, the cost of completeness (100% test coverage, full edge case handling, complete error paths) is 10-100x cheaper than with a human team. If the plan proposes a shortcut that saves human-hours but only saves minutes with CC+gstack, recommend the complete version. Boil the lake.

If the complexity check triggers (8+ files or 2+ new classes/services), proactively recommend scope reduction via AskUserQuestion ‚Äî explain what's overbuilt, propose a minimal version that achieves the core goal, and ask whether to reduce or proceed as-is. If the complexity check does not trigger, present your Step 0 findings and proceed directly to Section 1.

Always work through the full interactive review: one section at a time (Architecture ‚Üí Code Quality ‚Üí Tests ‚Üí Performance) with at most 8 top issues per section.

**Critical: Once the user accepts or rejects a scope reduction recommendation, commit fully.** Do not re-argue for smaller scope during later review sections. Do not silently reduce scope or skip planned components.

## Review Sections (after scope is agreed)

### 1. Architecture review
Evaluate:
* Overall system design and component boundaries.
* Dependency graph and coupling concerns.
* Data flow patterns and potential bottlenecks.
* Scaling characteristics and single points of failure.
* Security architecture (auth, data access, API boundaries).
* Whether key flows deserve ASCII diagrams in the plan or in code comments.
* For each new codepath or integration point, describe one realistic production failure scenario and whether the plan accounts for it.

**STOP.** For each issue found in this section, call AskUserQuestion individually. One issue per call. Present options, state your recommendation, explain WHY. Do NOT batch multiple issues into one AskUserQuestion. Only proceed to the next section after ALL issues in this section are resolved.

### 2. Code quality review
Evaluate:
* Code organization and module structure.
* DRY violations‚Äîbe aggressive here.
* Error handling patterns and missing edge cases (call these out explicitly).
* Technical debt hotspots.
* Areas that are over-engineered or under-engineered relative to my preferences.
* Existing ASCII diagrams in touched files ‚Äî are they still accurate after this change?

**STOP.** For each issue found in this section, call AskUserQuestion individually. One issue per call. Present options, state your recommendation, explain WHY. Do NOT batch multiple issues into one AskUserQuestion. Only proceed to the next section after ALL issues in this section are resolved.

### 3. Test review
Make a diagram of all new UX, new data flow, new codepaths, and new branching if statements or outcomes. For each, note what is new about the features discussed in this branch and plan. Then, for each new item in the diagram, make sure there is a JS or Rails test.

For LLM/prompt changes: check the "Prompt/LLM changes" file patterns listed in CLAUDE.md. If this plan touches ANY of those patterns, state which eval suites must be run, which cases should be added, and what baselines to compare against. Then use AskUserQuestion to confirm the eval scope with the user.

**STOP.** For each issue found in this section, call AskUserQuestion individually. One issue per call. Present options, state your recommendation, explain WHY. Do NOT batch multiple issues into one AskUserQuestion. Only proceed to the next section after ALL issues in this section are resolved.

### Test Plan Artifact

After producing the test diagram, write a test plan artifact to the project directory so `/qa` and `/qa-only` can consume it as primary test input (replacing the lossy git-diff heuristic):

```bash
eval $(~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-slug 2>/dev/null)
USER=$(whoami)
DATETIME=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
mkdir -p ~/.gstack/projects/$SLUG
```

Write to `~/.gstack/projects/{slug}/{user}-{branch}-test-plan-{datetime}.md`:

```markdown
# Test Plan
Generated by /plan-eng-review on {date}
Branch: {branch}
Repo: {owner/repo}

## Affected Pages/Routes
- {URL path} ‚Äî {what to test and why}

## Key Interactions to Verify
- {interaction description} on {page}

## Edge Cases
- {edge case} on {page}

## Critical Paths
- {end-to-end flow that must work}
```

This file is consumed by `/qa` and `/qa-only` as primary test input. Include only the information that helps a QA tester know **what to test and where** ‚Äî not implementation details.

### 4. Performance review
Evaluate:
* N+1 queries and database access patterns.
* Memory-usage concerns.
* Caching opportunities.
* Slow or high-complexity code paths.

**STOP.** For each issue found in this section, call AskUserQuestion individually. One issue per call. Present options, state your recommendation, explain WHY. Do NOT batch multiple issues into one AskUserQuestion. Only proceed to the next section after ALL issues in this section are resolved.

## CRITICAL RULE ‚Äî How to ask questions
Follow the AskUserQuestion format from the Preamble above. Additional rules for plan reviews:
* **One issue = one AskUserQuestion call.** Never combine multiple issues into one question.
* Describe the problem concretely, with file and line references.
* Present 2-3 options, including "do nothing" where that's reasonable.
* For each option, specify in one line: effort (human: ~X / CC: ~Y), risk, and maintenance burden. If the complete option is only marginally more effort than the shortcut with CC, recommend the complete option.
* **Map the reasoning to my engineering preferences above.** One sentence connecting your recommendation to a specific preference (DRY, explicit > clever, minimal diff, etc.).
* Label with issue NUMBER + option LETTER (e.g., "3A", "3B").
* **Escape hatch:** If a section has no issues, say so and move on. If an issue has an obvious fix with no real alternatives, state what you'll do and move on ‚Äî don't waste a question on it. Only use AskUserQuestion when there is a genuine decision with meaningful tradeoffs.

## Required outputs

### "NOT in scope" section
Every plan review MUST produce a "NOT in scope" section listing work that was considered and explicitly deferred, with a one-line rationale for each item.

### "What already exists" section
List existing code/flows that already partially solve sub-problems in this plan, and whether the plan reuses them or unnecessarily rebuilds them.

### TODOS.md updates
After all review sections are complete, present each potential TODO as its own individual AskUserQuestion. Never batch TODOs ‚Äî one per question. Never silently skip this step. Follow the format in `.claude/skills/review/TODOS-format.md`.

For each TODO, describe:
* **What:** One-line description of the work.
* **Why:** The concrete problem it solves or value it unlocks.
* **Pros:** What you gain by doing this work.
* **Cons:** Cost, complexity, or risks of doing it.
* **Context:** Enough detail that someone picking this up in 3 months understands the motivation, the current state, and where to start.
* **Depends on / blocked by:** Any prerequisites or ordering constraints.

Then present options: **A)** Add to TODOS.md **B)** Skip ‚Äî not valuable enough **C)** Build it now in this PR instead of deferring.

Do NOT just append vague bullet points. A TODO without context is worse than no TODO ‚Äî it creates false confidence that the idea was captured while actually losing the reasoning.

### Diagrams
The plan itself should use ASCII diagrams for any non-trivial data flow, state machine, or processing pipeline. Additionally, identify which files in the implementation should get inline ASCII diagram comments ‚Äî particularly Models with complex state transitions, Services with multi-step pipelines, and Concerns with non-obvious mixin behavior.

### Failure modes
For each new codepath identified in the test review diagram, list one realistic way it could fail in production (timeout, nil reference, race condition, stale data, etc.) and whether:
1. A test covers that failure
2. Error handling exists for it
3. The user would see a clear error or a silent failure

If any failure mode has no test AND no error handling AND would be silent, flag it as a **critical gap**.

### Completion summary
At the end of the review, fill in and display this summary so the user can see all findings at a glance:
- Step 0: Scope Challenge ‚Äî ___ (scope accepted as-is / scope reduced per recommendation)
- Architecture Review: ___ issues found
- Code Quality Review: ___ issues found
- Test Review: diagram produced, ___ gaps identified
- Performance Review: ___ issues found
- NOT in scope: written
- What already exists: written
- TODOS.md updates: ___ items proposed to user
- Failure modes: ___ critical gaps flagged
- Lake Score: X/Y recommendations chose complete option

## Retrospective learning
Check the git log for this branch. If there are prior commits suggesting a previous review cycle (e.g., review-driven refactors, reverted changes), note what was changed and whether the current plan touches the same areas. Be more aggressive reviewing areas that were previously problematic.

## Formatting rules
* NUMBER issues (1, 2, 3...) and LETTERS for options (A, B, C...).
* Label with NUMBER + LETTER (e.g., "3A", "3B").
* One sentence max per option. Pick in under 5 seconds.
* After each review section, pause and ask for feedback before moving on.

## Review Log

After producing the Completion Summary above, persist the review result:

```bash
eval $(~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-slug 2>/dev/null)
mkdir -p ~/.gstack/projects/$SLUG
echo '{"skill":"plan-eng-review","timestamp":"TIMESTAMP","status":"STATUS","unresolved":N,"critical_gaps":N,"mode":"MODE"}' >> ~/.gstack/projects/$SLUG/$BRANCH-reviews.jsonl
```

Substitute values from the Completion Summary:
- **TIMESTAMP**: current ISO 8601 datetime
- **STATUS**: "clean" if 0 unresolved decisions AND 0 critical gaps; otherwise "issues_open"
- **unresolved**: number from "Unresolved decisions" count
- **critical_gaps**: number from "Failure modes: ___ critical gaps flagged"
- **MODE**: FULL_REVIEW / SCOPE_REDUCED

## Review Readiness Dashboard

After completing the review, read the review log and config to display the dashboard.

```bash
eval $(~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-slug 2>/dev/null)
cat ~/.gstack/projects/$SLUG/$BRANCH-reviews.jsonl 2>/dev/null || echo "NO_REVIEWS"
echo "---CONFIG---"
~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-config get skip_eng_review 2>/dev/null || echo "false"
```

Parse the output. Find the most recent entry for each skill (plan-ceo-review, plan-eng-review, plan-design-review, design-review-lite). Ignore entries with timestamps older than 7 days. For Design Review, show whichever is more recent between `plan-design-review` (full visual audit) and `design-review-lite` (code-level check). Append "(FULL)" or "(LITE)" to the status to distinguish. Display:

```
+====================================================================+
|                    REVIEW READINESS DASHBOARD                       |
+====================================================================+
| Review          | Runs | Last Run            | Status    | Required |
|-----------------|------|---------------------|-----------|----------|
| Eng Review      |  1   | 2026-03-16 15:00    | CLEAR     | YES      |
| CEO Review      |  0   | ‚Äî                   | ‚Äî         | no       |
| Design Review   |  0   | ‚Äî                   | ‚Äî         | no       |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------+
| VERDICT: CLEARED ‚Äî Eng Review passed                                |
+====================================================================+
```

**Review tiers:**
- **Eng Review (required by default):** The only review that gates shipping. Covers architecture, code quality, tests, performance. Can be disabled globally with \`gstack-config set skip_eng_review true\` (the "don't bother me" setting).
- **CEO Review (optional):** Use your judgment. Recommend it for big product/business changes, new user-facing features, or scope decisions. Skip for bug fixes, refactors, infra, and cleanup.
- **Design Review (optional):** Use your judgment. Recommend it for UI/UX changes. Skip for backend-only, infra, or prompt-only changes.

**Verdict logic:**
- **CLEARED**: Eng Review has >= 1 entry within 7 days with status "clean" (or \`skip_eng_review\` is \`true\`)
- **NOT CLEARED**: Eng Review missing, stale (>7 days), or has open issues
- CEO and Design reviews are shown for context but never block shipping
- If \`skip_eng_review\` config is \`true\`, Eng Review shows "SKIPPED (global)" and verdict is CLEARED

## Unresolved decisions
If the user does not respond to an AskUserQuestion or interrupts to move on, note which decisions were left unresolved. At the end of the review, list these as "Unresolved decisions that may bite you later" ‚Äî never silently default to an option.

content.text: Good ‚Äî preamble clean, lake intro already seen. Let me do the eng review of the Phase 8 plan. I already have deep context from the CEO review, so I'll re-read the plan and TODOS, then work through systematically.

content.content:
1‚Üí# Phase 8: Growth & Retention ‚Äî Implementation Plan
     2‚Üí
     3‚Üí**Goal:** Transform Local Roots from a functional marketplace into a growth-ready platform with buyer retention loops, seller engagement analytics, organic acquisition via SEO, and hardened payout infrastructure.
     4‚Üí
     5‚Üí**North Star:** Increase completed pickups per week by building the systems that keep buyers coming back and help sellers see the value of the platform.
     6‚Üí
     7‚Üí**Architecture:** Go backend (new endpoints + cron jobs), Next.js App Router frontend (new pages + enhanced components), PostgreSQL (new queries, no schema changes expected), Resend (new email templates).
     8‚Üí
     9‚Üí---
    10‚Üí
    11‚Üí## Overview: What Exists vs. What's New
    12‚Üí
    13‚Üí| Area | Already Built | Phase 8 Adds |
    14‚Üí|------|--------------|-------------|
    15‚Üí| **Payouts** | Auto-transfer on pickup/no-show, payout history, summary per window | Transfer failure recovery, retry mechanism, aggregated payout dashboard, expected next payout |
    16‚Üí| **Analytics** | Subscriber count, revenue by cycle, pickup rate, payout history | Trend sparklines, review score integration, retention rate, subscriber cohort metrics, "last active" tracking |
    17‚Üí| **Re-engagement** | 11 transactional emails, pickup reminders, billing cron | Lapsed subscriber detection, win-back emails, post-pickup review prompt, subscription renewal nudges |
    18‚Üí| **Multi-location** | 1:N store‚Üípickup_locations schema, geo search | Seller multi-location management UI, buyer location picker on subscription checkout |
    19‚Üí| **SEO / Profiles** | Store detail page with generateMetadata, basic OG tags | Rich public profiles, city landing pages, structured data (JSON-LD), sitemap.xml, robots.txt, OG images |
    20‚Üí
    21‚Üí---
    22‚Üí
    23‚Üí## Priority Order
    24‚Üí
    25‚Üí1. **Buyer Re-engagement** ‚Äî Directly impacts retention (the #1 metric). Zero infrastructure exists.
    26‚Üí2. **Enhanced Seller Analytics** ‚Äî Keeps sellers engaged, shows them value of platform.
    27‚Üí3. **Public Store Profiles / SEO** ‚Äî Unlocks organic buyer acquisition channel.
    28‚Üí4. **Payout Hardening** ‚Äî Important but not blocking (transfers already work).
    29‚Üí5. **Multi-location UX** ‚Äî Lowest urgency (schema exists, few farmers need it at early stage).
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üí---
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üí## Task 1: Buyer Re-engagement ‚Äî Lapsed Subscriber Detection
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üí**Files:**
    36‚Üí- Create: `backend/internal/api/v1/internal_reengagement.go`
    37‚Üí- Modify: `backend/internal/scheduler/scheduler.go` (add cron job)
    38‚Üí- Modify: `backend/internal/email/templates.go` (add 3 new templates)
    39‚Üí- Modify: `backend/internal/httpx/handler.go` (register internal endpoint)
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üí### Step 1: Define "lapsed" criteria
    42‚Üí
    43‚ÜíA subscriber is considered lapsed when:
    44‚Üí- Status is `active` (not paused or canceled)
    45‚Üí- Their last picked-up order was 2+ cycles ago (based on plan cadence)
    46‚Üí- They have not been sent a re-engagement email in the last 14 days
    47‚Üí
    48‚Üí### Step 2: Add re-engagement tracking column
    49‚Üí
    50‚ÜíCreate migration `backend/migrations/XXXX_reengagement_tracking.sql`:
    51‚Üí```sql
    52‚Üí-- +goose Up
    53‚ÜíALTER TABLE subscriptions ADD COLUMN last_reengagement_email_at TIMESTAMPTZ;
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí-- +goose Down
    56‚ÜíALTER TABLE subscriptions DROP COLUMN last_reengagement_email_at;
    57‚Üí```
    58‚Üí
    59‚Üí### Step 3: Build lapsed subscriber query
    60‚Üí
    61‚Üí```sql
    62‚ÜíSELECT s.id, s.buyer_email, s.buyer_name, sp.title as plan_title,
    63‚Üí       sp.cadence, st.name as store_name, st.id as store_id
    64‚ÜíFROM subscriptions s
    65‚ÜíJOIN subscription_plans sp ON sp.id = s.plan_id
    66‚ÜíJOIN stores st ON st.id = s.store_id
    67‚ÜíWHERE s.status = 'active'
    68‚Üí  AND (s.last_reengagement_email_at IS NULL
    69‚Üí       OR s.last_reengagement_email_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '14 days')
    70‚Üí  AND NOT EXISTS (
    71‚Üí    SELECT 1 FROM orders o
    72‚Üí    WHERE o.subscription_id = s.id
    73‚Üí      AND o.status = 'picked_up'
    74‚Üí      AND o.picked_up_at > NOW() - (
    75‚Üí        CASE sp.cadence
    76‚Üí          WHEN 'weekly' THEN INTERVAL '14 days'
    77‚Üí          WHEN 'biweekly' THEN INTERVAL '28 days'
    78‚Üí          WHEN 'monthly' THEN INTERVAL '60 days'
    79‚Üí        END
    80‚Üí      )
    81‚Üí  )
    82‚ÜíLIMIT 50;
    83‚Üí```
    84‚Üí
    85‚Üí### Step 4: Add email templates
    86‚Üí
    87‚ÜíThree new templates in `templates.go`:
    88‚Üí1. **LapsedSubscriberNudge** ‚Äî "We miss you at {store_name}! Your next {plan_title} box is waiting."
    89‚Üí2. **PostPickupReviewPrompt** ‚Äî "How was your box from {store_name}?" (sent 2 hours after pickup confirmation)
    90‚Üí3. **UpcomingPickupExcitement** ‚Äî "Your {plan_title} box is ready tomorrow! Here's what to expect." (enhancement of existing reminder with richer content)
    91‚Üí
    92‚Üí### Step 5: Build cron job
    93‚Üí
    94‚ÜíAdd `RunReengagement()` to scheduler, running every 6 hours:
    95‚Üí- Query lapsed subscribers (limit 50 per run to avoid email bursts)
    96‚Üí- Send `LapsedSubscriberNudge` email
    97‚Üí- Update `last_reengagement_email_at`
    98‚Üí
    99‚Üí### Step 6: Add post-pickup review prompt
   100‚Üí
   101‚ÜíIn `pickup_execute.go`, after the pickup confirmation + payment receipt email, schedule a delayed review prompt:
   102‚Üí- Add `review_prompt_sent_at` column to orders (migration)
   103‚Üí- Cron job checks for orders picked up 2+ hours ago with no review and no prompt sent
   104‚Üí- Sends `PostPickupReviewPrompt` email with link to review page
   105‚Üí
   106‚Üí### Step 7: Tests
   107‚Üí
   108‚Üí- Test lapsed query logic with mock data (active sub, no recent pickups)
   109‚Üí- Test email template rendering
   110‚Üí- Test cron idempotency (re-running doesn't re-send)
   111‚Üí- Test edge cases: paused subs excluded, canceled subs excluded, recently engaged excluded
   112‚Üí
   113‚Üí### Step 8: Verification
   114‚Üí
   115‚Üí```bash
   116‚Üícd backend && go test ./...
   117‚Üí```
   118‚Üí
   119‚Üí---
   120‚Üí
   121‚Üí## Task 2: Enhanced Seller Analytics
   122‚Üí
   123‚Üí**Files:**
   124‚Üí- Modify: `backend/internal/api/v1/seller_analytics.go` (enhance response)
   125‚Üí- Modify: `frontend/src/app/seller/stores/[storeId]/analytics/page.tsx` (add visualizations)
   126‚Üí- Modify: `frontend/src/lib/seller-api.ts` (update types)
   127‚Üí
   128‚Üí### Step 1: Enhance analytics endpoint response
   129‚Üí
   130‚ÜíAdd to `AnalyticsResponse`:
   131‚Üí```go
   132‚Üítype AnalyticsResponse struct {
   133‚Üí    // ... existing fields ...
   134‚Üí
   135‚Üí    // New fields
   136‚Üí    RetentionRate      float64              `json:"retention_rate"`       // subscribers retained over last 30 days
   137‚Üí    AvgRating          *float64             `json:"avg_rating"`           // average review score (nullable)
   138‚Üí    ReviewCount        int                  `json:"review_count"`         // total reviews
   139‚Üí    SubscriberTrend    []TrendPoint         `json:"subscriber_trend"`     // last 12 weeks subscriber count snapshots
   140‚Üí    RevenueGrowthPct   *float64             `json:"revenue_growth_pct"`   // revenue change vs prior period
   141‚Üí    TopProducts        []TopProduct         `json:"top_products"`         // top 5 ordered products
   142‚Üí    NoShowRate         float64              `json:"no_show_rate"`         // no_show / total non-canceled
   143‚Üí}
   144‚Üí
   145‚Üítype TrendPoint struct {
   146‚Üí    Date  string `json:"date"`
   147‚Üí    Value int    `json:"value"`
   148‚Üí}
   149‚Üí
   150‚Üítype TopProduct struct {
   151‚Üí    Title    string `json:"title"`
   152‚Üí    Quantity int    `json:"quantity"`
   153‚Üí    Revenue  int    `json:"revenue_cents"`
   154‚Üí}
   155‚Üí```
   156‚Üí
   157‚Üí### Step 2: Add review aggregation query
   158‚Üí
   159‚Üí```sql
   160‚ÜíSELECT COALESCE(AVG(r.rating), 0), COUNT(r.id)
   161‚ÜíFROM reviews r WHERE r.store_id = $1;
   162‚Üí```
   163‚Üí
   164‚Üí### Step 3: Add retention rate calculation
   165‚Üí
   166‚Üí```sql
   167‚Üí-- Subscribers who were active 30 days ago and are still active
   168‚ÜíWITH cohort AS (
   169‚Üí    SELECT COUNT(*) as total
   170‚Üí    FROM subscriptions
   171‚Üí    WHERE store_id = $1 AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
   172‚Üí      AND status != 'canceled'
   173‚Üí)
   174‚ÜíSELECT
   175‚Üí    CASE WHEN cohort.total = 0 THEN 0
   176‚Üí    ELSE (
   177‚Üí        SELECT COUNT(*)::float / cohort.total
   178‚Üí        FROM subscriptions
   179‚Üí        WHERE store_id = $1 AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
   180‚Üí          AND status = 'active'
   181‚Üí    ) END as retention_rate
   182‚ÜíFROM cohort;
   183‚Üí```
   184‚Üí
   185‚Üí### Step 4: Add top products query
   186‚Üí
   187‚Üí```sql
   188‚ÜíSELECT oi.product_title, SUM(oi.quantity) as total_qty,
   189‚Üí       SUM(oi.price_cents * oi.quantity) as total_revenue
   190‚ÜíFROM order_items oi
   191‚ÜíJOIN orders o ON o.id = oi.order_id
   192‚ÜíWHERE o.store_id = $1 AND o.status = 'picked_up'
   193‚ÜíGROUP BY oi.product_title
   194‚ÜíORDER BY total_qty DESC
   195‚ÜíLIMIT 5;
   196‚Üí```
   197‚Üí
   198‚Üí### Step 5: Frontend ‚Äî Enhance analytics page
   199‚Üí
   200‚ÜíUpdate the analytics page to show:
   201‚Üí1. **Metric cards row** ‚Äî Active subscribers (with trend arrow), Pickup rate, Avg rating (stars), Revenue (with growth %)
   202‚Üí2. **Revenue by cycle** ‚Äî Keep existing table, add visual sparkline
   203‚Üí3. **Retention rate** ‚Äî Simple gauge or percentage display
   204‚Üí4. **Top products** ‚Äî Small ranked list
   205‚Üí5. **No-show rate** ‚Äî Percentage with context

   210‚Üí- Test edge cases: no reviews (null avg), no orders, new store with zero data
   211‚Üí- Frontend: `pnpm typecheck && pnpm lint`

   327‚Üí### Step 1: Track transfer failures

   329‚ÜíAdd migration for transfer attempt tracking:
   330‚Üí```sql
   331‚Üí-- +goose Up
   332‚ÜíALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN transfer_attempted_at TIMESTAMPTZ;
   333‚ÜíALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN transfer_error TEXT;
   334‚Üí
   335‚Üí-- +goose Down
   336‚ÜíALTER TABLE orders DROP COLUMN transfer_attempted_at;
   337‚ÜíALTER TABLE orders DROP COLUMN transfer_error;
   338‚Üí```
   339‚Üí
   340‚Üí### Step 2: Update transferToSeller to record failures
   341‚Üí
   342‚ÜíInstead of silently no-oping on failure, record the attempt:
   343‚Üí```go
   344‚Üífunc transferToSeller(...) error {
   345‚Üí    // ... existing logic ...
   346‚Üí    if err != nil {
   347‚Üí        // Record failure for retry
   348‚Üí        db.Exec(ctx, `UPDATE orders SET transfer_attempted_at = NOW(), transfer_error = $1 WHERE id = $2`,
   349‚Üí            err.Error(), orderID)
   350‚Üí        return err
   351‚Üí    }
   352‚Üí    // Record success
   353‚Üí    db.Exec(ctx, `UPDATE orders SET stripe_transfer_id = $1, transfer_attempted_at = NOW(), transfer_error = NULL WHERE id = $2`,
   354‚Üí        transfer.ID, orderID)
   355‚Üí    return nil
   356‚Üí}
   357‚Üí```
   358‚Üí
   359‚Üí### Step 3: Build retry cron job
   360‚Üí
   361‚Üí`RunTransferRetry()` ‚Äî runs every hour:
   362‚Üí- Finds orders with `transfer_attempted_at IS NOT NULL AND stripe_transfer_id IS NULL AND transfer_error IS NOT NULL`
   363‚Üí- Retries transfer up to 3 times (check attempt count or age)
   364‚Üí- After 3 failures, flag for manual intervention
   365‚Üí

   370‚Üí- "Processing" (amber) ‚Äî no transfer_id, attempted < 1 hour ago
   371‚Üí- "Failed ‚Äî Retrying" (red) ‚Äî has error, under retry limit
   372‚Üí- "Action needed" (red) ‚Äî exceeded retry limit
   373‚Üí

# Phase 8: Growth & Retention ‚Äî Implementation Plan

**Goal:** Transform Local Roots from a functional marketplace into a growth-ready platform with buyer retention loops, seller engagement analytics, organic acquisition via SEO, and hardened payout infrastructure.

**North Star:** Increase completed pickups per week by building the systems that keep buyers coming back and help sellers see the value of the platform.

**Architecture:** Go backend (new endpoints + cron jobs), Next.js App Router frontend (new pages + enhanced components), PostgreSQL (new queries, no schema changes expected), Resend (new email templates).

---

## Overview: What Exists vs. What's New

| Area | Already Built | Phase 8 Adds |
|------|--------------|-------------|
| **Payouts** | Auto-transfer on pickup/no-show, payout history, summary per window | Transfer failure recovery, retry mechanism, aggregated payout dashboard, expected next payout |
| **Analytics** | Subscriber count, revenue by cycle, pickup rate, payout history | Trend sparklines, review score integration, retention rate, subscriber cohort metrics, "last active" tracking |
| **Re-engagement** | 11 transactional emails, pickup reminders, billing cron | Lapsed subscriber detection, win-back emails, post-pickup review prompt, subscription renewal nudges |
| **Multi-location** | 1:N store‚Üípickup_locations schema, geo search | Seller multi-location management UI, buyer location picker on subscription checkout |
| **SEO / Profiles** | Store detail page with generateMetadata, basic OG tags | Rich public profiles, city landing pages, structured data (JSON-LD), sitemap.xml, robots.txt, OG images |

---

## Priority Order

1. **Buyer Re-engagement** ‚Äî Directly impacts retention (the #1 metric). Zero infrastructure exists.
2. **Enhanced Seller Analytics** ‚Äî Keeps sellers engaged, shows them value of platform.
3. **Public Store Profiles / SEO** ‚Äî Unlocks organic buyer acquisition channel.
4. **Payout Hardening** ‚Äî Important but not blocking (transfers already work).
5. **Multi-location UX** ‚Äî Lowest urgency (schema exists, few farmers need it at early stage).

---

## Task 1: Buyer Re-engagement ‚Äî Lapsed Subscriber Detection

**Files:**
- Create: `backend/internal/api/v1/internal_reengagement.go`
- Modify: `backend/internal/scheduler/scheduler.go` (add cron job)
- Modify: `backend/internal/email/templates.go` (add 3 new templates)
- Modify: `backend/internal/httpx/handler.go` (register internal endpoint)

### Step 1: Define "lapsed" criteria

A subscriber is considered lapsed when:
- Status is `active` (not paused or canceled)
- Their last picked-up order was 2+ cycles ago (based on plan cadence)
- They have not been sent a re-engagement email in the last 14 days

### Step 2: Add re-engagement tracking column

Create migration `backend/migrations/XXXX_reengagement_tracking.sql`:
```sql
-- +goose Up
ALTER TABLE subscriptions ADD COLUMN last_reengagement_email_at TIMESTAMPTZ;

-- +goose Down
ALTER TABLE subscriptions DROP COLUMN last_reengagement_email_at;
```

### Step 3: Build lapsed subscriber query

```sql
SELECT s.id, s.buyer_email, s.buyer_name, sp.title as plan_title,
       sp.cadence, st.name as store_name, st.id as store_id
FROM subscriptions s
JOIN subscription_plans sp ON sp.id = s.plan_id
JOIN stores st ON st.id = s.store_id
WHERE s.status = 'active'
  AND (s.last_reengagement_email_at IS NULL
       OR s.last_reengagement_email_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '14 days')
  AND NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM orders o
    WHERE o.subscription_id = s.id
      AND o.status = 'picked_up'
      AND o.picked_up_at > NOW() - (
        CASE sp.cadence
          WHEN 'weekly' THEN INTERVAL '14 days'
          WHEN 'biweekly' THEN INTERVAL '28 days'
          WHEN 'monthly' THEN INTERVAL '60 days'
        END
      )
  )
LIMIT 50;
```

### Step 4: Add email templates

Three new templates in `templates.go`:
1. **LapsedSubscriberNudge** ‚Äî "We miss you at {store_name}! Your next {plan_title} box is waiting."
2. **PostPickupReviewPrompt** ‚Äî "How was your box from {store_name}?" (sent 2 hours after pickup confirmation)
3. **UpcomingPickupExcitement** ‚Äî "Your {plan_title} box is ready tomorrow! Here's what to expect." (enhancement of existing reminder with richer content)

### Step 5: Build cron job

Add `RunReengagement()` to scheduler, running every 6 hours:
- Query lapsed subscribers (limit 50 per run to avoid email bursts)
- Send `LapsedSubscriberNudge` email
- Update `last_reengagement_email_at`

### Step 6: Add post-pickup review prompt

In `pickup_execute.go`, after the pickup confirmation + payment receipt email, schedule a delayed review prompt:
- Add `review_prompt_sent_at` column to orders (migration)
- Cron job checks for orders picked up 2+ hours ago with no review and no prompt sent
- Sends `PostPickupReviewPrompt` email with link to review page

### Step 7: Tests

- Test lapsed query logic with mock data (active sub, no recent pickups)
- Test email template rendering
- Test cron idempotency (re-running doesn't re-send)
- Test edge cases: paused subs excluded, canceled subs excluded, recently engaged excluded

### Step 8: Verification

```bash
cd backend && go test ./...
```

## Task 2: Enhanced Seller Analytics

**Files:**
- Modify: `backend/internal/api/v1/seller_analytics.go` (enhance response)
- Modify: `frontend/src/app/seller/stores/[storeId]/analytics/page.tsx` (add visualizations)
- Modify: `frontend/src/lib/seller-api.ts` (update types)

### Step 1: Enhance analytics endpoint response

Add to `AnalyticsResponse`:
```go
type AnalyticsResponse struct {
    // ... existing fields ...

    // New fields
    RetentionRate      float64              `json:"retention_rate"`       // subscribers retained over last 30 days
    AvgRating          *float64             `json:"avg_rating"`           // average review score (nullable)
    ReviewCount        int                  `json:"review_count"`         // total reviews
    SubscriberTrend    []TrendPoint         `json:"subscriber_trend"`     // last 12 weeks subscriber count snapshots
    RevenueGrowthPct   *float64             `json:"revenue_growth_pct"`   // revenue change vs prior period
    TopProducts        []TopProduct         `json:"top_products"`         // top 5 ordered products
    NoShowRate         float64              `json:"no_show_rate"`         // no_show / total non-canceled
}

type TrendPoint struct {
    Date  string `json:"date"`
    Value int    `json:"value"`
}

type TopProduct struct {
    Title    string `json:"title"`
    Quantity int    `json:"quantity"`
    Revenue  int    `json:"revenue_cents"`
}
```

### Step 2: Add review aggregation query

```sql
SELECT COALESCE(AVG(r.rating), 0), COUNT(r.id)
FROM reviews r WHERE r.store_id = $1;
```

### Step 3: Add retention rate calculation

```sql
-- Subscribers who were active 30 days ago and are still active
WITH cohort AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) as total
    FROM subscriptions
    WHERE store_id = $1 AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
      AND status != 'canceled'
)
SELECT
    CASE WHEN cohort.total = 0 THEN 0
    ELSE (
        SELECT COUNT(*)::float / cohort.total
        FROM subscriptions
        WHERE store_id = $1 AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
          AND status = 'active'
    ) END as retention_rate
FROM cohort;
```

### Step 4: Add top products query

```sql
SELECT oi.product_title, SUM(oi.quantity) as total_qty,
       SUM(oi.price_cents * oi.quantity) as total_revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN orders o ON o.id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.store_id = $1 AND o.status = 'picked_up'
GROUP BY oi.product_title
ORDER BY total_qty DESC
LIMIT 5;
```

### Step 5: Frontend ‚Äî Enhance analytics page

Update the analytics page to show:
1. **Metric cards row** ‚Äî Active subscribers (with trend arrow), Pickup rate, Avg rating (stars), Revenue (with growth %)
2. **Revenue by cycle** ‚Äî Keep existing table, add visual sparkline
3. **Retention rate** ‚Äî Simple gauge or percentage display
4. **Top products** ‚Äî Small ranked list
5. **No-show rate** ‚Äî Percentage with context

- Test edge cases: no reviews (null avg), no orders, new store with zero data

### Step 3: Create city landing pages

`/farms/[city]` ‚Äî server-rendered pages showing farms in a city:

### Step 4: Create dynamic sitemap

`frontend/src/app/sitemap.ts`:
```typescript
export default async function sitemap(): Promise<MetadataRoute.Sitemap> {
  const stores = await api.listStores();
  const cities = await api.listCities();
  return [
    { url: 'https://localroots.com', changeFrequency: 'weekly', priority: 1 },
    { url: 'https://localroots.com/stores', changeFrequency: 'daily', priority: 0.9 },
    ...cities.map(c => ({
      url: `https://localroots.com/farms/${c.city.toLowerCase()}`,
      changeFrequency: 'weekly' as const,
      priority: 0.8,
    })),
    ...stores.map(s => ({
      url: `https://localroots.com/stores/${s.id}`,
      changeFrequency: 'weekly' as const,
      priority: 0.7,
    })),
  ];
}
```

### Step 5: Create robots.ts

```typescript
export default function robots(): MetadataRoute.Robots {
  return {
    rules: { userAgent: '*', allow: '/', disallow: ['/seller/', '/buyer/', '/auth/'] },
    sitemap: 'https://localroots.com/sitemap.xml',
  };
}
```

On `/stores` and city landing pages, show:

## Task 4: Payout Hardening

**Files:**
- Modify: `backend/internal/api/v1/order_helpers.go` (add retry logic)
- Create: `backend/internal/api/v1/internal_transfer_retry.go` (retry cron)
- Modify: `backend/internal/scheduler/scheduler.go` (register retry job)
- Modify: `frontend/src/app/seller/stores/[storeId]/analytics/page.tsx` (payout status UI)

### Step 1: Track transfer failures

Add migration for transfer attempt tracking:
```sql
-- +goose Up
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN transfer_attempted_at TIMESTAMPTZ;
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN transfer_error TEXT;

-- +goose Down
ALTER TABLE orders DROP COLUMN transfer_attempted_at;
ALTER TABLE orders DROP COLUMN transfer_error;
```

### Step 2: Update transferToSeller to record failures

Instead of silently no-oping on failure, record the attempt:
```go
func transferToSeller(...) error {
    // ... existing logic ...
    if err != nil {
        // Record failure for retry
        db.Exec(ctx, `UPDATE orders SET transfer_attempted_at = NOW(), transfer_error = $1 WHERE id = $2`,
            err.Error(), orderID)
        return err
    }
    // Record success
    db.Exec(ctx, `UPDATE orders SET stripe_transfer_id = $1, transfer_attempted_at = NOW(), transfer_error = NULL WHERE id = $2`,
        transfer.ID, orderID)
    return nil
}
```

### Step 3: Build retry cron job

`RunTransferRetry()` ‚Äî runs every hour:
- Finds orders with `transfer_attempted_at IS NOT NULL AND stripe_transfer_id IS NULL AND transfer_error IS NOT NULL`
- Retries transfer up to 3 times (check attempt count or age)
- After 3 failures, flag for manual intervention

### Step 4: Frontend ‚Äî Show transfer status more clearly

On the payout history page:
- "Transferred" (green) ‚Äî has transfer_id
- "Processing" (amber) ‚Äî no transfer_id, attempted < 1 hour ago
- "Failed ‚Äî Retrying" (red) ‚Äî has error, under retry limit
- "Action needed" (red) ‚Äî exceeded retry limit

### Step 5: Tests

- Test transfer failure recording
- Test retry logic doesn't double-transfer
- Test retry limit enforcement

# TODOS

Deferred work items tracked for future implementation.

---

## P2: "Next pickup" date badge on store cards

**What:** Show "Next pickup: Sat Mar 22" on each store card in `/stores` browse results and city landing pages.

**Why:** Growth audit flagged pickup date as a top purchase signal ‚Äî knowing *when* you can get food drives action. Currently buyers must click into a store to see pickup dates.

**Pros:** Surfaces purchase-intent data earlier in the funnel, reduces unnecessary click-throughs, increases qualified traffic to store detail pages.

**Cons:** Requires an additional query per store (next published pickup window) ‚Äî minor performance cost.

**Context:** Backend needs to include `next_pickup_date` in the store list response by querying upcoming published windows for each store. Frontend renders it as a badge on `StoreCard`. The `pickup_windows` table already has `start_at` and `status` columns. Could use a lateral join or subquery in the existing `ListStores` query.

**Effort:** S (human: ~4h / CC: ~10 min)

**Priority:** P2

**Depends on / blocked by:** None. Can be done independently of Phase 8.

**Source:** CEO plan review 2026-03-18, Phase 8 deferred item.

---

# ENG PLAN REVIEW ‚Äî Phase 8: Growth & Retention

## Step 0: Scope Challenge

**Existing code reuse:** The plan correctly extends all existing infrastructure (email client, cron scheduler, analytics endpoint, geo search, photo upload). No unnecessary rebuilding. **Good.**

**Minimum set:** The 5 original tasks are well-prioritized. The 6 CEO-accepted expansions add ~30 min CC time total and compound retention value. No scope bloat.

**Complexity check:** ~20 files total across 5 tasks + expansions. But each task is independently scoped at 3-6 files and could ship as a separate PR. **No smell ‚Äî this is a multi-task plan, not a monolithic change.**

**TODOS cross-reference:** The "next pickup badge" TODO could fold into Task 3 (store card enhancements), but the user explicitly deferred it in the CEO review. Respected.

**Completeness check:** The plan has three engineering gaps that need resolution before implementation. Let me walk through them.

---

## Section 1: Architecture Review

The overall architecture is sound ‚Äî independent features extending proven patterns. I found 3 issues worth discussing.

**Obvious fixes (no decision needed, will note for implementation):**
- Task 4: Add `transfer_retry_count INT DEFAULT 0` column to track attempt count explicitly (not inferred from age)
- Task 3: City landing page URLs need slugification (`San Francisco` ‚Üí `san-francisco`). Cities endpoint should return a `slug` field.
- Task 3: Sitemap should use `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` env var instead of hardcoded `https://localroots.com`
- Task 1: Add index `CREATE INDEX idx_orders_sub_status_pickup ON orders(subscription_id, status, picked_up_at)` for the re-engagement query

Now the genuine decisions:

questions.question: ENG ISSUE 1: The retention rate query is mathematically wrong. The plan computes 'what % of subscribers created >30 days ago are currently active.' That's a snapshot, not a retention rate. True 30-day retention is: 'of subscribers who were active 30 days ago, what % are still active today?' The system doesn't have point-in-time status snapshots, so we can't know who was active 30 days ago directly. But we CAN approximate using cancel/pause timestamps. RECOMMENDATION: Choose A because it's correct with data we already have (canceled_at, paused_at exist), and the query is only marginally more complex. Completeness: 9/10 vs 5/10.

options.label: A) Approximate from lifecycle events (Recommended)
options.description: Retention ‚âà 1 - (canceled_in_30d / (active + canceled_in_30d)). Uses existing canceled_at column. Correct metric. (human: ~2h / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 9/10

options.label: B) Keep current query, rename to 'Active Rate'
options.description: Rename from 'retention_rate' to 'mature_active_rate'. Honest about what it measures. Simpler. (human: ~0 / CC: ~0). Completeness: 5/10

options.label: C) Add snapshot table for true cohort tracking
options.description: New cron job records daily subscriber counts. Most correct, but adds infrastructure. Ocean territory for Phase 8. (human: ~3 days / CC: ~30 min). Completeness: 10/10

questions.question: ENG ISSUE 2: The plan says 'last 12 weeks subscriber count snapshots' for the trend sparkline, but there's no snapshot infrastructure. You can't just query 'how many subscribers did I have 8 weeks ago?' without either (A) computing it from lifecycle events or (B) storing periodic snapshots. Computing from events means: for each of 12 weeks, count subscriptions where created_at <= week_end AND (canceled_at IS NULL OR canceled_at > week_end). This is a single CTE query but hits the subscriptions table 12 times. At current scale (<100 subs per store) this is trivial. RECOMMENDATION: Choose A because it avoids new infrastructure while the data is small enough to compute on the fly. Completeness: 8/10.

options.label: A) Compute from lifecycle events (Recommended)
options.description: Single CTE with generate_series for 12 weeks, count subs per week using created_at/canceled_at. No new tables. Fast at current scale. (human: ~3h / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 8/10

options.label: B) Add weekly snapshot cron
options.description: New metrics_snapshots table + weekly cron that records subscriber count. Most correct, future-proof, but adds infrastructure for a feature that may change. (human: ~1 day / CC: ~15 min). Completeness: 10/10

Both correct choices ‚Äî lifecycle events keep the architecture simple while the data is small. No new tables, just smarter queries.

**Architecture section complete.** 2 decisions resolved, 4 obvious fixes noted. Moving on.

---

## Section 2: Code Quality Review

**DRY concern:** Analytics queries will be used in both `seller_analytics.go` (API endpoint) and the new seller digest email cron. The plan should extract shared query functions (e.g., `StoreMetrics(ctx, db, storeID)`) to avoid duplicating SQL across both codepaths. **Obvious fix ‚Äî no decision needed.**

**Cron organization:** Going from 2 ‚Üí 8 jobs. The scheduler file should group by domain with clear comments. **Noted for implementation.**

**CEO expansion integration:** The 6 accepted expansions need task-level specs added to the plan before implementation. Specifically:
- `box_updates` table + CRUD endpoints + seller UI + buyer display + email integration (Task 6)
- `waitlist` table + form endpoint + notification cron (Task 7)
- Seller digest email template + weekly cron (Task 8)
- Milestone detection logic + email templates (Task 9)
- OG image route (fold into Task 3)

**No decisions needed in this section.** All are implementation notes. Moving on.

questions.question: ENG ISSUE 3: The milestone detection needs a tracking mechanism to prevent re-sending the same milestone email. For example, after sending the '10th pickup!' email, the system must remember that milestone was sent. Two options for tracking. RECOMMENDATION: Choose A because it's explicit, queryable, and follows your preference for explicit over clever. The join table costs ~5 extra lines of Go code. Completeness: 10/10.

options.description: Add milestones_sent JSONB to subscriptions. Store sent milestones as ['5','10']. Simpler schema, but harder to query and violates 1NF. (human: ~30min / CC: ~3 min). Completeness: 6/10

# Test Plan
Generated by /plan-eng-review on 2026-03-18
Branch: main
Repo: Local-Roots

## Affected Pages/Routes

### Backend Endpoints (new)
- `POST /v1/internal/reengagement` ‚Äî trigger lapsed subscriber email scan
- `GET /v1/cities` ‚Äî list cities with active stores and slugs
- `POST /v1/seller/stores/{storeId}/box-updates` ‚Äî create "what's in the box" content
- `GET /v1/stores/{storeId}/box-updates` ‚Äî public: list box updates for a store
- `POST /v1/waitlist` ‚Äî add email to waitlist with geo coordinates
- `POST /v1/seller/stores/{storeId}/locations` ‚Äî add pickup location
- `PUT /v1/seller/stores/{storeId}/locations/{locationId}` ‚Äî update location
- `DELETE /v1/seller/stores/{storeId}/locations/{locationId}` ‚Äî remove location

### Backend Endpoints (modified)
- `GET /v1/seller/stores/{storeId}/analytics` ‚Äî new fields: retention_rate, avg_rating, review_count, subscriber_trend, revenue_growth_pct, top_products, no_show_rate
- `transferToSeller()` ‚Äî now records errors and retry count

### Frontend Pages (new)
- `/farms/{city}` ‚Äî city landing page with stores in that area
- `/sitemap.xml` ‚Äî dynamic sitemap
- `/robots.txt` ‚Äî robots file
- `/stores/{storeId}/opengraph-image` ‚Äî dynamic OG image

### Frontend Pages (modified)
- `/seller/stores/{storeId}/analytics` ‚Äî new metric cards, retention, trends, top products
- `/seller/stores/{storeId}/settings` ‚Äî "Pickup Locations" management section
- `/stores/{storeId}` ‚Äî JSON-LD structured data, box updates display
- `/stores` ‚Äî waitlist form on empty search results

## Key Interactions to Verify
- Seller posts "what's in the box" content on dashboard ‚Üí appears on buyer-facing store page
- Buyer searches for farms, finds nothing ‚Üí sees email capture form ‚Üí submits ‚Üí sees "you're on the list"
- Buyer with lapsed subscription ‚Üí receives re-engagement email (check inbox after 6h cron)
- Buyer picks up box ‚Üí receives review prompt email ~2 hours later
- Buyer hits 5th pickup ‚Üí receives milestone celebration email
- Seller views analytics page ‚Üí sees retention rate, avg rating, subscriber trend, top products
- Seller receives Monday digest email with weekly metrics summary
- Transfer fails ‚Üí shows "Processing" ‚Üí retry succeeds ‚Üí shows "Transferred"
- Transfer fails 3x ‚Üí shows "Action needed" on payout history
- Seller adds second pickup location ‚Üí buyer sees location picker on subscribe form
- Store page shared on social media ‚Üí shows branded OG image with store name + price

## Edge Cases
- Re-engagement email: subscriber pauses between query and send (should be skipped)
- Re-engagement email: subscriber who was nudged, picked up once, then lapsed again (14-day cooldown)
- Milestone email: subscriber cancels and re-subscribes (count cumulative pickups)
- Analytics: brand new store with zero data (all metrics show empty/zero state)
- Transfer retry: order was refunded after capture but before transfer (skip, don't retry)
- City landing page: city name with spaces ("San Francisco" ‚Üí slug "san-francisco")
- Waitlist notification: store goes live but in a different city than waitlist entry (match by radius, not city name)
- OG image: store has no hero image and no reviews (render branded fallback)
- Location delete: location has future pickup windows (block delete, show error)
- Box update: seller posts then deletes (should clean up, not leave orphan)

## Critical Paths
- Full re-engagement flow: active sub misses 2 cycles ‚Üí cron detects ‚Üí email sent ‚Üí timestamp updated ‚Üí not re-sent within 14 days
- Full transfer retry flow: pickup confirmed ‚Üí transfer fails ‚Üí error recorded ‚Üí retry cron picks up ‚Üí retries with same idempotency key ‚Üí succeeds ‚Üí transfer_id saved
- Full milestone flow: buyer's 10th pickup ‚Üí cron detects ‚Üí checks milestone_emails table ‚Üí not yet sent ‚Üí sends email ‚Üí records in milestone_emails ‚Üí next run skips
- Full waitlist flow: buyer signs up on empty search ‚Üí store goes live in area ‚Üí cron matches by radius ‚Üí notification sent ‚Üí buyer subscribes

content.text:
Test plan written. Moving on.

---

## Section 4: Performance Review

| Concern | Severity | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Re-engagement NOT EXISTS query** | Med | Add index `orders(subscription_id, status, picked_up_at)` ‚Äî noted in architecture fixes |
| **Subscriber trend 12-week CTE** | Low | Single CTE with `generate_series`. At <100 subs/store, sub-millisecond. Add comment noting this should migrate to snapshots at 1000+ subs |
| **Analytics endpoint: 4+ additional queries** | Med | Combine review aggregation + retention + no-show rate into a single CTE. Keep top products separate (different table). Reduces round trips from 4 to 2 |
| **OG image generation** | Low | Add `Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600` header. Images only change when store data changes |
| **City aggregation** | Low | Simple GROUP BY on a small dataset. Fine at current scale |
| **Sitemap generation** | Low | Queries all stores + cities. Fine at <100 stores. Add ISR/caching note for 10K+ |

**No decisions needed.** All performance items are implementation notes ‚Äî combine queries, add indexes, add cache headers. Moving on.

---

## Failure Modes

| Codepath | Failure Mode | Test? | Error Handling? | User Impact | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RunReengagement | DB connection pool exhausted | Y | Y (log + exit) | Silent | No |
| RunReengagement | Resend API rate limit | Y | Y (stop batch) | Silent | No |
| RunTransferRetry | Stripe charge already refunded | Y | Y (skip + record) | "Action needed" | No |
| RunTransferRetry | Stripe idempotency key collision | N | Y (Stripe handles) | Silent | No |
| RunMilestoneCheck | Two cron runs detect same milestone | Y | Y (unique constraint on milestone_emails) | Silent | No |
| RunWaitlistNotify | Waitlist email bounces | N | Y (Resend handles) | Silent | No |
| RunSellerDigest | Seller has no email (shouldn't happen) | Y | Y (skip) | Silent | No |
| City landing page | City slug collision (two "Springfield"s) | N | N | **Wrong results** | **WARNING** |
| OG image | ImageResponse timeout (large images) | N | Y (Next.js default) | Fallback | No |

**1 WARNING:** City slug collision. Two cities named "Springfield" in different states would collide at `/farms/springfield`. Resolution: use `city-region` as the slug (e.g., `springfield-il`, `springfield-mo`). **Obvious fix ‚Äî noted for implementation.**

**0 CRITICAL GAPS.** All failure modes have either test coverage or framework-level error handling.

---

## Required Outputs

### NOT in scope
- Event tracking infrastructure (PostHog/Mixpanel)
- Cohort analysis / metrics snapshots table
- Referral program
- Box customization
- Seller CRM / subscriber messaging
- EBT/SNAP payment support
- Next pickup badge on store cards (in TODOS.md)
- Review stars on store cards (skipped)
- Seller welcome email drip (skipped)

### What already exists
| Sub-problem | Existing code | Reused? |
|---|---|---|
| Email sending | Resend client + 11 templates | Yes |
| Cron scheduling | In-process scheduler (2 jobs) | Yes |
| Analytics queries | seller_analytics.go | Yes ‚Äî extend |
| Store pages + SEO meta | generateMetadata() + OG tags | Yes ‚Äî enhance |
| Geo search | Haversine in public.go | Yes ‚Äî reuse for cities + waitlist |
| Photo upload | Supabase integration | Yes ‚Äî reuse for box update photos |
| Transfer logic | transferToSeller() | Yes ‚Äî extend with error recording |

### TODOS.md updates

No new TODOs to propose. The existing "next pickup badge" TODO was reviewed ‚Äî it could fold into Task 3 but the user explicitly deferred it. No changes.

### Diagrams needed in code

| File | Diagram Type | Content |
|---|---|---|
| `internal_reengagement.go` | Flow comment | Cron flow: query ‚Üí filter ‚Üí send ‚Üí update |
| `internal_transfer_retry.go` | State diagram | Transfer states: pending ‚Üí retrying ‚Üí succeeded / escalated |
| `scheduler.go` | Job registry comment | Table of all 8 jobs with intervals |

---

## Implementation Notes (consolidated fixes)

1. **Retention rate:** Use lifecycle approximation: `1 - (canceled_in_30d / (active + canceled_in_30d))`
2. **Subscriber trend:** CTE with `generate_series` computing from `created_at`/`canceled_at`
3. **Transfer retry tracking:** Add `transfer_retry_count INT DEFAULT 0` column
4. **City slugs:** Use `city-region` format (`austin-tx`) to avoid collisions
5. **Sitemap domain:** Use env var, not hardcoded URL
6. **Milestone tracking:** New `milestone_emails` table
7. **Analytics DRY:** Extract shared `StoreMetrics()` query function
8. **Performance:** Combine analytics sub-queries into CTEs; add `orders(subscription_id, status, picked_up_at)` index
9. **OG images:** Add `Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600`
10. **CEO expansions:** Need task-level specs added to plan (box_updates, waitlist, seller digest, milestones, OG images)

---

```
- Code Quality Review: 0 issues (2 implementation notes)
- Test Review: 1 decision resolved (milestone tracking), ~50 test cases diagrammed
- Performance Review: 0 issues (6 implementation notes)
- NOT in scope: written (9 items)
- What already exists: written (7 reusable subsystems)
- TODOS.md updates: 0 new items proposed
- Failure modes: 0 critical gaps, 1 WARNING (city slug collision ‚Äî fixed)
- Lake Score: 3/3 recommendations chose complete option
```

```

|                    REVIEW READINESS DASHBOARD                       |
+====================================================================+
| Review          | Runs | Last Run            | Status    | Required |
|-----------------|------|---------------------|-----------|----------|
| Eng Review      |  1   | 2026-03-18 16:00    | CLEAR     | YES      |
| CEO Review      |  1   | 2026-03-18 15:30    | CLEAR     | no       |
| Design Review   |  0   | ‚Äî                   | ‚Äî         | no       |
```

`‚òÖ Insight ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ`
**Retention rate vs. active rate:** This is a common analytics trap. "What % of my subscribers are active?" (a snapshot) is very different from "What % of subscribers who were active last month are still active?" (a retention rate). The first can look great while hiding massive churn ‚Äî if you're acquiring new subscribers fast enough, the percentage stays high even as old ones leave. The lifecycle-event approximation (`1 - churn_rate`) gives you the real signal. This distinction matters enormously for product-market fit evaluation.

**City slug collision:** The `springfield` problem is a real-world version of the "namespace collision" pattern. Any time you derive a unique identifier from user-provided data (city names, usernames, file names), assume collisions exist. The `city-region` slug pattern (`springfield-il`) is the standard solution ‚Äî same approach GitHub uses for `owner/repo`.
`‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ`

---

Both reviews are **CLEARED**. The plan is ready for implementation. The 10 consolidated implementation notes should be applied when writing the code.

Would you like to start implementing, or would you like a `/plan-design-review` for the UI-facing changes (analytics page, city landing pages, OG images) first?
